title: Git Basics — init, add, commit, log, diff
description: Understanding Git's data lifecycle and file status state machine is the foundation of version control mastery.

sections.title: Initializing a Repository
sections.description:
git init creates a new .git directory with the object store, refs, HEAD pointer, and config. git clone copies an existing remote repo including all history. Use git init --bare for server-side repos and git clone --depth 1 for shallow copies of large repos.

git init                  # new local repo
git clone <url>           # clone remote
git init my-project --bare # bare repo (server)

sections.title: Staging & Committing
sections.description:
The staging area (index) lets you craft commits incrementally. git add stages content; git reset unstages. git commit -m creates a snapshot. Use git commit --amend to update the last commit without creating a new one.

git add file.txt          # stage single file
git add .                 # stage all changes
git reset file.txt        # unstage
git commit -m "message"   # commit staged
git commit -a -m "msg"    # stage+commit tracked files

sections.title: Viewing History
sections.description:
git log is the primary tool for exploring commit history. Use flags like --oneline, --graph, --decorate for compact visualization. git show displays a single commit's diff and metadata.

git log --oneline --graph --decorate -10
git log --author="name" --since="2 weeks"
git show HEAD             # latest commit details

sections.title: Inspecting Changes
sections.description:
git diff shows unstaged changes; git diff --staged shows staged changes. git diff HEAD compares working tree to last commit. Use git blame to trace line-by-line authorship.

git diff                  # unstaged changes
git diff --staged         # staged changes
git diff HEAD~2..HEAD     # range diff
git blame file.txt        # line annotations

sections.title: Undoing Changes
sections.description:
git restore (modern) or git checkout -- file discards working tree changes. git restore --staged unstages. git reset --soft moves HEAD while keeping changes; git reset --hard discards everything.

git restore file.txt          # discard working changes
git restore --staged file.txt # unstage
git reset --soft HEAD~1       # undo commit, keep changes
git reset --hard HEAD~1       # undo commit, discard changes

details: Git is a content-addressable filesystem. Every object is stored by its SHA-1 hash. The working tree is what you see in your editor. The index (staging area) is a binary file at .git/index that tracks paths, timestamps, and object references. A commit stores a tree object (directory snapshot) plus parent pointers, author/committer info, and a message. The three-tree architecture (HEAD, index, working tree) is the key to understanding all Git operations.
cheatSheet:
Core Commands

  • git init / git clone — start local or copy remote. Prefer --depth 1 for CI.
  • git add -p — interactive staging. Staff+ engineers never use bare git add . without review.
  • git commit -v — verbose commit shows the diff in the editor. Write commits as subject\n\nbody.
  • git log --oneline --graph --decorate — the daily driver for history inspection.
  • git restore --staged / git restore — modern undo (not checkout).

Staff+ Patterns

  • Use git commit --amend --no-edit to fix the last commit — never reset and recommit.
  • Prefer git restore over git checkout for file ops. checkout is overloaded and confuses juniors.

Watch Outs

  • git reset --hard discards everything with no warning. git stash first if unsure.
  • git commit -a stages ALL tracked files — easy to accidentally commit debug logs.

Interview Angle

Git basics appear as live-coding warm-ups. Interviewers watch for: do you use git add -p (indicates experience) or blindly git add .? Explain the three-tree architecture to demonstrate depth.
